name: Ariadne
description: Ariadne, daughter of King Minos of Crete and Pasiphae, is a pivotal figure in Greek mythology, primarily known for her role in the myth of Theseus and the Minotaur. She is often depicted as a compassionate and resourceful princess who defied her father to aid Theseus in his quest to slay the monstrous Minotaur, a creature confined within the labyrinth built by Daedalus. Falling in love with Theseus upon his arrival in Crete, Ariadne provided him with a ball of thread (clew) so he could navigate the labyrinth and find his way back out after confronting the Minotaur. She also gifted him a magical sword to defeat the beast. After Theseus successfully killed the Minotaur and escaped the labyrinth, he fled Crete with Ariadne. However, he later abandoned her on the island of Naxos. Different accounts explain this abandonment, some suggesting it was divine intervention from Dionysus, who desired Ariadne for himself, while others attribute it to Theseus simply tiring of her. On Naxos, Ariadne was found by Dionysus, the god of wine and revelry, who made her his wife. Their marriage was a joyous occasion, and she bore him several children. Ariadne's crown was later placed among the stars as the constellation Corona Borealis. She is often viewed as a symbol of love, betrayal, and eventual redemption. Her story highlights themes of courage, sacrifice, and the complexities of human relationships, and her association with Dionysus connects her to themes of fertility, joy, and divine favor. Ariadne's legacy extends beyond her involvement with Theseus, solidifying her as a significant figure in Greek mythology in her own right.
subtitle: The princess who aided Theseus.
achievements: Aided Theseus in slaying the Minotaur
Her crown was placed among the stars

keyMyths.title: Ariadne and the Minotaur
keyMyths.description: Ariadne fell in love with Theseus and helped him navigate the labyrinth by giving him a ball of thread to unwind, allowing him to trace his steps back after killing the Minotaur. She also provided him with a sword to use against the beast. This act sealed Theseus's success and their escape from Crete.

keyMyths.title: Abandonment on Naxos
keyMyths.description: After fleeing Crete with Theseus, Ariadne was abandoned by him on the island of Naxos. The reasons for this vary in different accounts, ranging from divine intervention to Theseus simply leaving her behind. This event marked a turning point in Ariadne's life.

keyMyths.title: Marriage to Dionysus
keyMyths.description: On Naxos, Ariadne was discovered by Dionysus, who fell in love with her and made her his wife. Their marriage was a joyous occasion, and she became a consort of the god of wine, fertility, and revelry. This union brought her happiness and elevation to divine status.

sources.author: Apollodorus
sources.description: A comprehensive account of Greek mythology.
sources.text: A comprehensive account of Greek mythology.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Epic poem featuring a brief mention of Ariadne's fate.
